summary:
Laduguer is the duergar god of toil, weapons-craft, magical forging, harsh protection, discipline, suspicion, and survival without comfort. In the Realms, he is the chief god of the gray dwarves and a member-in-exile of the Morndinsamman: still dwarven, still kin to the divine clan, but cast out from Moradin’s household and worshiped most strongly by duergar communities. Realms sources describe him as a god of weapons-crafting, magic, and the duergar, with his exile from the Morndinsamman treated as central to his myth.

He should not be written as a simple god of evil dwarves. Laduguer is harsher and more specific: the divine patron of a people who survived betrayal, enslavement, exile, deep-earth cruelty, and their own hardening into tyranny. His gifts are discipline, endurance, secrecy, forged weapons, magical craft, gray patience, and the refusal to be ruled by surface judgment. His sins are slavery, loveless labor, spiritual starvation, conquest, paranoia, and the belief that survival justifies every cruelty.

To dwarves outside duergar culture, Laduguer is often the Exile, a warning against pride, rebellion, unforgivable craft, and kinship severed from mercy. To many duergar, he is the Gray Protector: the only god who did not abandon them, the harsh master who demands labor because labor kept them alive, and the divine smith who turns suffering into weapons.
tax:
Laduguer belongs under the Dwarven Pantheon, but as an estranged or exiled member rather than a healthy hearth-member of Moradin’s divine household. The Morndinsamman entry should keep him inside the extended dwarven divine taxonomy because Realms lore usually still counts Laduguer and Deep Duerra as members-in-exile rather than wholly foreign powers.

His parentage and placement should preserve the dual account of his exile. Non-duergar dwarven traditions claim he committed grave crimes against his divine kin and was saved from Moradin’s wrath only by Berronar’s temperance. Duergar traditions reverse the moral emphasis, portraying him as a righteous innovator whose philosophy offended Moradin and led to unjust banishment. The catalogue should not resolve this completely. The contradiction is part of Laduguer’s identity.

He should be separated from Moradin by craft ethic. Moradin’s forge creates people, souls, communities, and honorable works. Laduguer’s forge produces weapons, tools, survival systems, and disciplined hardness. Moradin tempers through trial toward wholeness; Laduguer tempers through deprivation toward utility. Both are smith-gods, but their moral temperature is different.

He should be separated from Abbathor by motive. Abbathor is greed: treasure hoarded because possession itself has become corruptive desire. Laduguer is not primarily greed, though duergar society under his shadow may be grasping and cruel. His center is harsh labor, control, weaponized craft, and survival through domination.

He should be separated from Deep Duerra by scope. Laduguer is the elder duergar patron of toil, craft, protection, and grim law. Deep Duerra is more strongly tied to conquest, expansion, psionics, and imperial duergar ambition. Realms sources identify her as Laduguer’s daughter and a duergar-associated power, likewise exiled from the Morndinsamman.
ap:
Laduguer should appear as a gray, severe dwarven smith-warrior: bald or close-shorn, iron-bearded or ash-bearded, with skin like worked stone, eyes like banked coals, and hands scarred by both forge and chain. He is not radiant in Moradin’s fashion. His divinity is weight, heat, silence, pressure, and the ring of a hammer that sounds more like sentence than song.

His common symbols should include a broken crossbow bolt on a shield, a gray hammer, a locked forge, an anvil in darkness, a weapon unfinished because it can still be improved, chains used as tools and punishments, and a shield that protects only those who obey. The broken bolt motif is widely associated with Laduguer in secondary summaries of his Realms presentation.

His temples should be austere forge-halls, weapon foundries, sealed chapels, guard-barracks, slave-worked manufactories, subterranean citadels, and hidden sanctums behind armories. They are not places of song or communal feasting. The air should feel hot, metallic, ordered, and joyless. Every surface has purpose. Every tool has a place. Every worshiper has a duty.

Priests of Laduguer should dress practically: gray mail, forge aprons, dark stone holy symbols, weapon belts, soot marks, and tools that double as ritual implements. Their vestments should look like work gear made sacred by use rather than ornament. Even ceremonial objects should be able to survive battle, cave-in, and interrogation.

Miracles of Laduguer should look like brutal competence: a hammer striking with impossible precision, a weapon refusing to break, a slave chain glowing with divine law, an enemy’s armor exposing its weakest rivet, a forge burning in air too thin for fire, or a duergar worker continuing long after exhaustion should have broken them.
eco:
Laduguer’s cult belongs to duergar society as its divine spine: labor, hierarchy, weapon production, secrecy, suspicion, and communal survival enforced by fear. The chief god of the duergar is identified as Laduguer in Realms lore, with his worship tied back to Clan Duergar and the old divine patronage of the gray dwarves.

His shrines thrive in places where craft and coercion are inseparable: deep mines, foundries, slave pens, siege workshops, armories, fortress-cities, hidden arsenals, and clan halls where every citizen owes labor. A Laduguerite settlement should feel productive and spiritually starved. Nothing is wasted, but little is celebrated. Children learn craft as duty before craft as pride. Failure is corrected, punished, and remembered.

His worship is practical and repetitive. Daily labor can be prayer. Improving a weapon can be liturgy. Enduring pain without complaint can be devotion. Guarding the hold, exposing weakness, disciplining a worker, completing a quota, forging a magical blade, and refusing outside mercy may all be framed as holy acts.

Public ceremony should be sparse. Duergar religion under Laduguer should avoid festivals of release, because release weakens the structure he sanctifies. Prayer before work, inspection after work, confession of inefficiency, punishment rites, weapon consecration, oath-binding, and silent remembrance of exile are more appropriate than song, feast, or open joy.

His ecology also includes resentment. Laduguer’s worship preserves the duergar grievance against other dwarves and against the divine household that cast him out. This grievance gives his cult cohesion, but it also traps it. Every forge becomes proof of survival; every chain becomes a reminder that domination is safer than trust.
beh:
Laduguer behaves as a taskmaster, protector, judge, exile, and divine smith who never praises enough. He grants strength, endurance, weapon-skill, magical craft, and grim protection, but he demands improvement without comfort. A worshiper who survives is told to work harder. A weapon that succeeds is reforged to be better. A city that endures is ordered to prepare for siege.

He is not impulsive. His cruelty is structured. He prefers law, hierarchy, discipline, punishment, efficiency, and long memory. He does not waste rage when pressure will do. His wrath is the locked gate, the withheld rest, the impossible quota, the hammer brought down at exactly the weakest point.

Toward duergar, he is protective in the harshest sense. He defends them from enemies who would enslave, consume, deceive, or erase them, but his protection often strengthens the very habits that make duergar society cruel. He teaches them never to rely on pity, never to forget betrayal, never to leave a weapon unready, and never to let labor become soft.

Toward other dwarves, Laduguer is grievance embodied. He may despise Moradin’s line for hypocrisy, weakness, sentiment, or remembered injustice. Yet he remains recognizably dwarven: he values craft, endurance, clan survival, stone, metal, and oath. That unresolved kinship is what makes him more interesting than an outside tyrant.

Toward his priests, he should be exacting and dangerous. A cleric who wastes resources, praises comfort, shows public weakness, or permits disobedience may lose favor. A cleric who improves a process, exposes a flaw, forges a weapon of consequence, or protects a duergar hold through ruthless discipline may receive visions in sparks, hammer-rhythm, or metal fatigue.
traitsText:
**Deity Type**. Treat Laduguer as a dwarven deity and duergar patron, not as a generic underworld god. He is part of the extended Morndinsamman structure but should be marked as exiled, estranged, or hostile to Moradin’s central household.

**Realms Status**. In Realms-primary usage, Laduguer is the chief god of the duergar and a member-in-exile of the Morndinsamman. His exile is central and should appear in any serious entry.

**Alignment Tone**. Lawful evil is the cleanest tonal center: law without mercy, craft without joy, protection through domination, and survival converted into hierarchy.

**Core Portfolios**. Duergar, weapons-craft, magical weapons, toil, discipline, protection, harsh labor, grim endurance, subterranean survival, suspicion, and exile. Some tables may broaden him into craft-magic and gray dwarven law.

**Holy Symbol**. A broken crossbow bolt on a shield is the standard symbol to preserve. It should evoke defense, grievance, and a missile stopped or broken against duergar resolve.

**Worshiper Base**. Duergar are the primary worshipers. Other dwarves may study him as a warning, appease him in rare cases, or fall into his cult through exile, bitterness, weapons obsession, or hatred of Moradin’s priesthood.

**Clerical Pattern**. Laduguer’s priests should be overseers, smiths, weapon-enchanters, disciplinarians, military chaplains, engineers, interrogators, and guardians of duergar orthodoxy. They should rarely be pastoral comforters.

**Temple Pattern**. Forge-chapels, armories, barracks, prison-workshops, deep citadels, sealed smithies, and command shrines. Worship should be integrated into labor and production.

**Offerings**. Finished weapons, improved tools, captured enemy arms, hours of unpaid labor, repaired fortifications, magical components, broken symbols of rival dwarven gods, and acts of endurance.

**Taboos**. Wasted labor, mercy that weakens the hold, public celebration that disrupts duty, unfinished weapons, unguarded secrets, reliance on outsiders, sentimental forgiveness of enemies, and disobedience to rightful duergar hierarchy.

**Common Domains**. In 3.5e-style use, Evil, Law, Magic, Protection, Craft, War, or Metal are reasonable depending on available domain lists. In Pathfinder-style use, Artifice, Evil, Law, Protection, War, Earth, Toil, or similar custom subdomains are appropriate.
abilities:
**Gray Forge Blessing** (Su). Laduguer grants insight into weapons, armor, mechanisms, and magical craft. A worshiper may identify a flaw, strengthen a tool, sharpen a weapon, or improve a forge process through grim divine focus.

**Taskmaster’s Endurance** (Su). Laduguer’s faithful can endure fatigue, hunger, pain, and oppressive labor beyond ordinary limits. This blessing should be useful but morally uncomfortable: it helps a community survive while making exploitation easier to sanctify.

**Weapon-Oath Consecration** (Su). A weapon forged or dedicated under Laduguer may gain power against enemies of the duergar, oathbreakers, rebels, or those who threaten the hold. The consecration should favor discipline and purpose over heroic flourish.

**Shield of the Exile** (Su). Laduguer protects those who stand apart, are cast out, or hold a gate against overwhelming enemies. The blessing may grant defensive bonuses, resistance to fear, or strength when cut off from allies.

**Chain-Law Mandate** (Su). Laduguer’s darker miracles bind, restrain, command, or punish. Chains, manacles, quotas, commands, and labor-oaths may become spiritually charged. Use this as a sign of his lawful evil nature, not as a neutral protection power.

**Hammer of Correction** (Su). Failure becomes visible under Laduguer’s gaze. A weak rivet, lazy worker, false report, flawed enchantment, or treacherous oath may reveal itself through sparks, cracking metal, heatless pain, or a hammer-tone only the faithful hear.

**No Praise, Only Improvement** (Su). A Laduguerite blessing may turn success into further compulsion. After victory or completed craft, the recipient gains a temporary bonus to the next related task but suffers unease, dissatisfaction, or divine pressure until the improvement is made.

**Grim Invisibility** (Sp or Su). Because duergar tradition strongly features stealth, secrecy, and hidden survival, Laduguer’s servants may receive concealment, invisibility, or silence-like blessings when guarding, ambushing, escaping, or protecting a forge-secret. This should support duergar identity without replacing Deep Duerra’s psionic emphasis.

**Exile’s Rebuke** (Su). Against priests or agents of rival dwarven powers, Laduguer may manifest as anti-hearth force: extinguished comfort, cracked oath-stones, bitter forge smoke, or a weapon that refuses Moradinite blessing. Use sparingly to emphasize divine feud.

**Labor Without Song** (Su). A site consecrated to Laduguer may suppress fatigue penalties while also suppressing morale, celebration, and emotional release. Workers endure longer, but joy drains from the hall.
bg:
Laduguer is most useful when written as a tragic and dangerous dwarven god rather than a flat villain. He is what happens when craft loses warmth, protection loses mercy, and survival becomes a theology of domination.

Forgotten Realms lore gives him strong placement as both duergar chief deity and exiled member of the Morndinsamman. This lets him serve several story roles at once: patron of gray dwarf society, divine explanation for duergar harshness, theological rival to Moradin, and unresolved kin-wound within dwarven religion.

In campaigns, Laduguer can appear wherever dwarven virtues have been twisted but not abandoned. A fortress that prizes readiness over life may drift toward him. A weaponsmith who values perfection more than conscience may hear his hammer. A clan exiled unjustly may find comfort in his myth, then slowly become harsher than its enemies. A duergar city may invoke him sincerely as protector while also using his doctrine to justify slavery and cruelty.

He should create uncomfortable questions. Was Laduguer’s exile deserved? Did Moradin’s household abandon the gray dwarves, or did Laduguer lead them away? Can duergar craft be redeemed without rejecting the god who preserved it? Is endurance holy when it is forced? Is protection still protection when it chains the protected?

The best Laduguer stories keep both halves visible: he is a protector of duergar survival, and he is a tyrant-god of loveless labor. Removing either half makes him less useful.
note:
Theme: exile, gray craft, weapons-forging, toil, harsh protection, duergar survival, and law without mercy.

Use Laduguer as the estranged duergar power of the Dwarven Pantheon. His place in the Morndinsamman should be marked as member-in-exile rather than ordinary harmonious member. Realms lore preserves competing accounts of his banishment: non-duergar dwarves describe grave crimes, while duergar traditions frame him as a rejected innovator and protector.

Core Realms points to preserve: chief god of the duergar; god of weapons-crafting, magic, and the duergar; exiled from the Morndinsamman; closely associated with Deep Duerra as the other major duergar power; holy symbol of a broken crossbow bolt on a shield.

Quality target: deity-level entry comparable to the Dwarven Pantheon entry, with emphasis on Forgotten Realms duergar lore, exile from Moradin’s household, grim craft, theological ambiguity, and campaign usability.
